items.q: What is overextension in child language?
items.correct: When a child applies a word more broadly than adults do — e.g. calling all four-legged animals 'dog' before learning distinct categories.
items.misconception: Overextension is when children use very long words they don't understand.
items.random_wrong: Overextension is when children speak too loudly.
items.why: Overextension is about semantic categories, not word length or volume. It shows children are forming hypotheses about meaning, then refining them — a key window into acquisition.

items.q: What is the difference between voiced and voiceless sounds?
items.correct: Voiced sounds are produced with vocal-fold vibration (/b/, /d/, /g/, /z/); voiceless sounds without it (/p/, /t/, /k/, /s/). You can feel the difference by putting a hand on your throat.
items.misconception: Voiced sounds are loud and voiceless sounds are whispered.
items.random_wrong: Voiced sounds use the lips; voiceless sounds use the tongue.
items.why: Voicing is about vocal-fold vibration, not volume. Whisper has no voicing at all but isn't silent. Students confuse phonation with loudness, missing how voicing distinguishes minimal pairs (pin/bin).

items.correct: A group of words from a similar area of meaning — e.g. 'whisper, scream, mumble, shout' all belong to the semantic field of vocalisation.
items.misconception: A semantic field is a list of synonyms for one word.
items.random_wrong: A semantic field is a field of study like physics or biology.
items.why: Synonyms share meaning closely; semantic fields are broader, including related but distinct words. 'Hot' and 'cold' belong to the same semantic field (temperature) but aren't synonyms.

items.q: How many letters in the PEEL plan?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
